title: Paḍilehaṇa Vidhi (Fifty Points / Pacāsa Bol)
summary: This is the set of fifty short contemplations a practitioner recites mindfully while inspecting the cloth used in the rite (the muhapattī, or mouth-cloth) and the body, before settling into worship. As the eyes and fingers check the cloth fold by fold for any tiny living being, the mind runs through fifty resolutions — twenty-five virtues to 'take up' (ādaruṁ) and twenty-five faults to 'let go' (pariharuṁ) — so that physical care for life is matched by inner cleansing. It turns a routine act of inspection into a compact examination of conscience, sealed with a confession of any harm done in thought, word, or deed.
ritualPlacement: In the Daivasika (Devasi) pratikramaṇa this comes in the opening sāmāyika-portion, right after Karemi Bhante (the equanimity vow, the 1st Āvaśyaka) and before the Dvādaśāvarta Vāndaṇā (the twelve-gesture guru-veneration, the 3rd Āvaśyaka). It is a vidhi-sutra — procedural contemplation that accompanies the physical paḍilehaṇā (inspection) of cloth and body when one takes/settles a sāmāyika. Because the other pratikramaṇas build on the Devasi base, this inspection belongs to the shared 'take a sāmāyika' procedure that Rāi, Pakkhī, Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī reuse rather than reprint.
authorAndSource: — No individual author is named in the source book (Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit, Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.); it is a traditional contemplative formula attached to the mindful inspection (pratilekhanā) of garments. The doctrinal lists it strings together (the three rāgas, the six leśyās, the three gāravas, the three śalyas, the four kaṣāyas, the six jīva-classes) are standard Śvetāmbara categories drawn from the Āgamic/Karmagrantha tradition, not the invention of this text.
structureArc: Though printed as a single piece, the formula is two balanced halves of twenty-five points each, plus a closing confession. The first twenty-five are things to 'take up' or 'guard against losing': hold sūtra-artha-tattva (scripture, meaning, reality) in the heart, then shed the three faith-deluding karmas (samyaktva-, miśra-, mithyātva-mohanīya), the three attachments (kāma-, sneha-, dṛṣṭi-rāga), embrace true deva-guru-dharma while rejecting false ones, embrace jñāna-darśana-cāritra (knowledge, faith, conduct) while shunning their violation (virādhanā), and adopt the three guptis (restraints of mind, speech, body) while dropping the three daṇḍas (their misuse). The second twenty-five are faults to 'let go': the disturbing emotions hāsya-rati-arati and śoka-bhaya-jugupsā, the three dark leśyās (kṛṣṇa, nīla, kāpota), the three gāravas (pride in wealth, taste, comfort), the three śalyas (deceit-, craving-, false-faith 'thorns'), the four passions krodha-māna-māyā-lobha, and finally the harm done to the six classes of living beings (earth-, water-, fire-, air-, plant-, and mobile-bodied). The whole closes: whatever wrong has occurred, by mind, speech, and body — micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ.
recurringImagery: The organizing motif is a deliberate pairing that runs the length of the text: ādaruṁ ('I take up / honor') versus pariharuṁ ('I forsake / renounce'). Each inspected fold of cloth becomes a moment to affirm one good and abandon one fault, so the outward act of looking for hidden life mirrors an inward act of looking for hidden defilement. A second pattern is the recurring triad — the points come almost entirely in groups of three (three mohanīyas, three rāgas, three guptis, three leśyās, three gāravas, three śalyas), a mnemonic rhythm that lets the reciter keep count to twenty-five and twenty-five while the hands work. The closing six (the ṣaṭ-kāya, six classes of life) ground the whole meditation back in its original purpose: non-harm to the smallest beings.
keyThemes: Mindful inspection (pratilekhanā) as non-violence in practice
Pairing of virtues to adopt and faults to renounce
Renunciation of passions, pride, and inner 'thorns'
Protection of the six classes of living beings
Sealing self-examination with micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ

glossary.definition: The mindful visual-and-tactile inspection of cloth and body for any living being before use; the act this formula accompanies.
A 'point' or spoken item of contemplation; here, the fifty resolutions (pacāsa bol).
The small mouth-cloth inspected and held during the rite to guard subtle aerial life and speech.
'To take up, honor, adopt' — the verb governing the twenty-five virtues to be embraced.
'To forsake, renounce' — the verb governing the twenty-five faults to be abandoned.
Deluding karma; here in its faith-deluding forms — samyaktva-, miśra-, and mithyātva-mohanīya.
Attachment; the three named are kāma- (sensual), sneha- (affectional), and dṛṣṭi- (dogmatic) attachment.
The 'three jewels' — right knowledge (jñāna), right faith (darśana), and right conduct (cāritra) — to be adopted; their harm is virādhanā.
Restraint or guarding of mind, speech, and body (mano-, vacana-, kāya-gupti); their misuse is the three daṇḍas.
The 'color' or tint of the soul's mental disposition; the three dark ones — kṛṣṇa (black), nīla (blue), kāpota (grey) — are renounced.
Self-indulgent pride: ṛddhi- (in wealth/status), rasa- (in taste), and sātā- (in comfort) gārava.
An inner 'thorn' obstructing liberation: māyā- (deceit), nidāna- (binding desire for reward), and mithyādarśana- (false faith) śalya.
The four core passions — anger (krodha), pride (māna), deceit (māyā), and greed (lobha).
The six classes of embodied life — earth-, water-, fire-, air-, plant-, and mobile-bodied beings — whose injury (virādhanā) is confessed.
'May my wrong-doing be in vain' — the formula of confession that retracts and atones for the fault, here sealing the whole contemplation.

howToRead: A newcomer can read it simply as a checklist for the soul — twenty-five good qualities to welcome and twenty-five faults to release while inspecting the cloth — and rest in the closing apology, micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ. An advanced practitioner will recognize each triad as a doorway into a full doctrinal category (the mohanīyas, leśyās, gāravas, śalyas, kaṣāyas, and ṣaṭ-kāya) and may meditate on each in turn as the inspection proceeds.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim text of the fifty bol and its placement in the Devasi sāmāyika-portion (the single golden source).

sources.title: Tattvārtha Sūtra (Umāsvāti), ch. 6–9
sources.consultedFor: Standard definitions of leśyā, kaṣāya, gupti, and the categories of karmic bondage referenced in the formula.

notes: The source book titles this 'Paḍilehaṇa Vidhi (50 bol)'; the same fifty-point contemplation is widely known in Śvetāmbara practice as the 'muhapattī nā 50 bol' recited during inspection of the mouth-cloth — the two are the same practice, named by its procedural role here. The internal point-counts (running totals of 25 + 25 = 50) follow the book's own numbering, with the opening 'sūtra-artha-tattva' line counted as one bol and most remaining lines as triads or pairs. No individual author or redactor is attested for this formula in the book; the URL for the source book is left blank as it is an offline print edition. Spellings such as 'apūkāya' (for ap-kāya) reflect the book's verbatim orthography and should not be normalized.
